reference.title: GitHub Settings Pack
reference.description: Guidance for configuring repository settings on GitHub to enforce quality and security. The settings described here should be applied during repository instantiation and checked regularly. Agents must adapt the values to the organisation’s policies but should not leave critical protections disabled.

list.item: Require pull‑request reviews before merging into protected branches. At least one approving review should be required. Dismiss stale reviews when the PR changes.

Require status checks to pass before merging. Configure required checks to include formatting, linting, type checking, unit tests, integration tests, accessibility checks and security scans. Do not allow bypassing failed checks.
Enable signed commits and require signed tags if your organisation mandates cryptographic verification of provenance.
Restrict who can push to protected branches. Only designated maintainers and trusted automation should have direct push rights.
Enable linear history to prevent merge commits if the repository prefers a rebase and merge workflow.

section.title: Code owners and review assignment
section.content: Define a `CODEOWNERS` file mapping file patterns to responsible reviewers. GitHub will automatically request reviews from these owners when relevant files are changed. Use fine‑grained patterns to ensure subject matter experts see the changes. Avoid assigning individual names; instead refer to teams or functional roles (e.g., `@org/maintainers`).

list.item: Enable Dependabot alerts and Dependabot security updates to automatically detect and patch vulnerable dependencies.
Enable secret scanning and push protection so that committed secrets are detected and blocked before merging.
Enable private vulnerability reporting and create a `SECURITY.md` file with disclosure instructions.
Enable CodeQL or equivalent static analysis for supported languages. These checks help detect security flaws beyond dependency issues.

section.title: Issue, discussion and project management
section.content: Configure issue templates, pull‑request templates and discussions to guide contributors. Enable GitHub Projects if the repository benefits from backlog tracking. Use labels for priority, type, risk and area, and enforce triage to keep the backlog manageable.
